transcript.text: Well, I'm also, Andy, nice to meet you.
You as well.
I lead customer operations for GrowthX.
So I have scheduled these feedback calls in line with Marcel.
So we're kind of trading off, taking them.
But I'm sort of in a team that's thinking about process optimization and deliverables specifically for the team that's our client-facing team.
And I know you worked with us for a little while, and you're rolling off the partnership now.

So I think this call will be a great opportunity just for us to learn about kind of where we achieved what you were looking for, where we missed the mark, and also just learn about kind of why you started working with us and how that partnership evolved.
Does that all sound good?
Yep, sounds good.
Perfect.
And yeah, there's no like, you know, no wrong answers.
We won't be offended.
And it's certainly not going to be like any sort of like sales or rescue pitch, just like really a feedback call.
So, you know, anything that you want to share with us is going to be valuable for us to learn going forward.
Cool.
Well, I would love to just start with kind of how you like came to GrowthX and what your goals were in working with us as you started out.
Yeah.
So my understanding is that there might have been either a connection between our CEO and somebody from your leadership team or our CEO heard about GrowthX from one of his kind of founder friends.
And for the idea.
And I think, you know, we have felt for a little while that we needed to modernize and scale our content program using, you know, more AI tools.
techniques.
And obviously that's right up your alley.
So that's why we brought you in.
think, you know, both to kind of learn from the pros, but also, you know, to kind of jumpstart and hopefully scale out organic traffic efforts.
Awesome.
And were you thinking at all about, were there specific KPIs or goals in using the AI that you had as you started out?
Yeah, I mean, there weren't any like quantifiable metrics or anything, you know, there were several specific projects and kind of specific tactics that we wanted to roll out.
And I think, you know, part of the challenge that probably your team had with us is that it was a bit of a shifting landscape.
You know, we had sort of just launched a new website.
We had just, you know, our CEO had just kind of ad-coded a bunch of stuff.
But in-
In general, we definitely wanted some guidance on some of the in-flight projects that we already had.
then we were looking to kind of get repeatable and scalable, I guess, AI-generated traffic assets is kind of how I would probably describe it.
How would you define the AI-generated traffic assets?
Because, yeah, are you thinking like multiple pieces of content or things that can be reused?
Yeah, I mean, I think we were open to both traditional long-form editorial content as well as more kind of the PSEO tool type assets.
Okay.
I think the main thing was moving faster with quality content.
And, you know, the goal was that your team and your expertise and your workflows would do that, would help us do that.
And, think it's super cool.
Thank
Okay.
And so in getting started or in the, I mean, I think we sort of just got started and I know it took a little while.
So how would you describe the feedback?
How would you describe the onboarding and like feedback that you have on that?
Yeah, no, I mean, I think it was strong.
Like I said, there, there was a bit of a star because we kind of had, you know, competing priorities.

And I think initially, a lot of the ask was around kind of guidance on like our new site, or like a new project that we were working on, as opposed to hitting the ground running with, you know, concrete content projects that your team was working on, on our behalf.

But, you know, I think the onboarding was good, like bigger picture, really, I mean, I know people, it's kind of stereotypical, but like, it really was a matter of like, we, we needed to bring this in house, ultimately, you know, we, we need to bring, you know, kind of the
All content workflows in-house, but think that's the case for a few reasons.
One, and I know I'm kind of getting ahead of probably your question on this, but I think it contextualizes all of my responses.
And it's the case for a few reasons.
One, subject matter expertise.
We are of the belief in the...
We are a pretty technical...
We sell a pretty technical product.
We have a very technical audience.
And we think there's a relatively high bar for quality that needs to be met.
And we need our internal people, I think, to be a bit more involved.
And it's very possible for, obviously, growthx to consult our SMEs, but I think we have a bit more control of the process from an editorial content quality standpoint.
I think from a PSEO standpoint, like, the best PSEO content we can produce would be...
Very much based on our internal data, as opposed to publicly accessible resources.
And I think that, again, it's like possible for growthx to do that, but it's tricky.
It's just communicating data, like figuring out where to look.
It's more difficult.
And so I think like, you know, the real value add we could add to PSEO is like internal data.
And I think just like, you know, the bigger picture of, you know, flexibility and timing and this and that, like everything pointed in the direction of, we need to manage this in-house.
We also have a very, you know, just another reason for it being in-house.
And I'm sure this is probably one of the pieces of feedback that your team would have is that like, we have a very non-traditional content publishing process.
We don't have a CMS.
And so like ordinarily, growthx would plug into a CMX, new CMS.
And I think you would be able to like publish more rapidly.
For us, like.

All of our content assets live in a GitHub repo, and I have to go in and publish in Markdown and code, and so it just makes, again, basically because of almost everything, from the technical nature of our product to our competitive advantage from a content perspective being some of our in-house data to our non-traditional publishing platform, it's all, it's, I guess, just become clear over the course of engagement, but, like, we really need to have this in-house, and it's just going to be very inefficient at this stage, you know, for us to try to figure out the best way to work with an external agency.
Yeah, well, okay, so I'm curious, have you started the process of, like, starting to generate this stuff on your own, and...
Yeah, so I have been using AI in some of my content, but it's been kind of more of, more...
Like, 40% of it, rather than 100% of it.
So, you know, like having ChatGPT do deep research on a topic, and then I'll treat that essentially like the first draft I get from a freelance writer.
I have not got to a point where I'm comfortable having AI do 90% of it, which I think is more kind of your style.
And so, I think on certain topics, maybe, but like, I just, and I don't know that this is like, it's not that I wouldn't be comfortable.
I think it's just, right now, for the topics that like, I've written about, that's been more the workflow.
Again, not necessarily opposed to having AI do more of it, and certainly with like, PSEO content, like, not opposed at all.
Like, if it's, you know, like the CVE pages that you have created for us, like, that's totally good.
I think it's for like, longer form editorial, opinionated perspective content, like, you know, I think.
Thank
That's kind of the philosophy right now.
Yeah.
Okay.
That's helpful.

I think, and how are you thinking, like, in terms of PSEO and setting up your CMS, like, in identifying those bottlenecks, maybe I'll call them, and working with us, has that driven you to have a strategy shift in your approach for that stuff going forward, or how are you, yeah, solving those problems?
Not necessarily.
There's continued internal conversations.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.

I mean, I think in general, like, we started the process to be a bit, you know, eyes wide open, and, you we've learned a lot over it, and I think, you know, just the biggest learning is that for both philosophical...
For logical and logistical reasons, like writing it in-house, it just made the most sense.
Yeah.
Obviously, if we were convicted of that in the first place, we wouldn't have gone down this path.
But that was, I think, just where the team netted out.
Yeah.
I mean, that's totally fair.
And I think, yeah, if you have the resources and the ability to leverage all the tools that we're sort of connecting, then certainly you should do it.
And I know that publishing, especially without a CMS on our end, can be complex.
So if that sounds like it was a blocker, no matter what.
Okay.
Is there anything else, like any other feedback or things like generally just thinking about, like, leveraging an agency that you would want us to keep in mind?
Yeah.
Like working with other partners?
Would you work with us again?
Yeah.
Um.
I would.
I think the, I appreciated kind of the extra layer of QA that your team did upon my request, like to sanity check some of the code snippets that were introduced in articles.

My thought is that like, and I guess if I had, and I don't, this isn't like directly, directly related to our decision, obviously, but as I've explained, you know, it was, it was really an in-house thing.
But my thought is that the pendulum will start to swing back to a point where craftsmanship and quality really shine through and companies really do start to reprioritize those.
And so I think as we look toward the next year, two years, like, you know, I, I think, and this might be wrong, that the AI doing 40, 50% of it is probably where I'm leaning as far as the mix.
And so I think that just for.

Companies in really technical industries with very kind of technical, sophisticated demand buyers, like your potential ability in the future to offer a true subject matter expert would be a pretty compelling value add.
Especially if you start to see that just hypothetically, like, you know, security companies are really reaching out to partner with you.

And if you can come back to those companies and say, you know, whereas historically our workflow has been AI doing 95% and a human doing kind of the quick QA, but the human doing the QA is not an expert.
It's more kind of Google search, Google research based.

And if you were to say, okay, now AI is doing 85%, but we're having, you know, a true security expert come and spend, you know, doing a 20 minute check on each article, plus our standard human doing the, you know, QA for 20 minutes.
It's like.
It's
That would certainly, I think, elevate confidence in your client base as we, and again, I might be wrong.
You know, it might just be the case that, you know, the craftsmanship angle ends up not being as important because AI gets to a point where, you know, the quality bar is so high.
Yeah.
You know, to me, though, that's where my current thinking is.
So, yeah, if you're starting to see clusters of companies in specific industries express interest, the ability to offer a subject matter expert in that industry would be very powerful, I think.
Yeah.
And for, you're thinking more for, like, editorial QA than, like, sort of marketing strategy writ large.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
There is a, I think it's more, I mean, there is a world where it's, if you have somebody who's, like, kind of a unicorn person who both is technical.
Google Ann gets content that they might advise on strategy.
I think that's unlikely.
And I think that's something where even from a control perspective, you might have your customers a little bit reluctant to fully give that up.
I think probably first and foremost, it's on the QA side.
And then, you know, again, kind of the next level is probably more like a strategic thought partner.
Like if you have somebody who's like really up to date on trends and stuff, then that's great.
But I think that the low hanging group is just probably a subject matter expert who can assist in up leveling quality.
Yeah.
Yeah.
That makes a lot of sense.
I think like it's interesting.
A lot of what you're saying is really interesting just given where we are.
As a company, because right now, we're, we just transitioned over to our own, like, internal platform that's running these workflows, and it's run on our own code base.
We're, like, being really specific about the prompts, and we're starting to, oh no, am I freezing again?
Yeah, you're going a bit in and out.
But maybe you're back.
All right.
Well, let me just show you something really briefly, because I think down the line, it might be interesting.

You might be interested just to see how we're evolving that thinking, because I think you're totally right, that, like, having deep company context and, like, industry understanding and also, like, deeper technical understanding is valuable.
Well, I think we're getting to the point in the tech where we can.
Start to input those controls with, like, really specific prompting and get much better content.
I don't think we're, we might not be at the place where your bar is yet.
Let me just share my screen.
Well, you say that you're now, you kind of have, you're managing all of your workflows in-house.
Like, how is that contrast to your previous process?
Yeah, so before we were leveraging AirOps to run our workflows.

So our code base was linked, like, AirOps was linking different parts of our code base that would ping various, like, the various AI systems at each stage that we were leveraging to write article generation or keyword analysis.
And we're using, obviously, different LLMs for each of those because they're all, like, some of them are better at things than others.
Like, we're just discovering right now that Manus is, like, really good at, like, deep research for, like, articles.
Okay.
And sort of more, better and generate a lot, a lot more like content unprompted in terms of competitive intelligence and.
That was Manus, broke up again.
Yeah, Manus.
Yeah.
And, and so, you know, to generate, what we're doing now is we have, we're starting to keep in our, for each client, the artifacts that we had before that we were just putting into Aerofs as prompts.

But these are like evolving bodies of work about like each company that we can edit, we can view history and like kind of call back to different evolutions of how a brand is changing, how company context might like be evolving.
I'm thinking about artifacts in terms of the personas.
So as you develop, like you might hone in on, you know, pain points, decision drivers, like your ICP might change every two to three months.
And we can really keep that as a company artifact and put it into every piece, every article we generate, every piece of research we're putting together and then writing guidelines.
Right.
So tone, voice, language, like things that you should say, like a list of things that you never say or do not use or things that are just wrong that you should never reference on a technical front.
Things like that, that can start to, you know, with some basic guardrails on the technical side, help create, help like create more of a structure, ladder for the AI to.
For content that really needs that technical bar.
And I'm not saying that we're there yet.
I think like the deeper that we get in developing these artifacts and, you know, we can have like links to different CTAs.

We can have examples of thought leaders that you always want to write in their tone of voice or like they're using or like leverage different parts of the like different pages on either reference competitors or never reference competitors in doing our deep research, like really point to different parts of the Internet writ large.
We're like we should and shouldn't be exploring.
We can like upload call context and put it into these artifacts like we can get really, really deep so that then when we go into our article generation, you know, we're taking that assignment breach.
Brief, taking deep research, and all of these are like really core artifacts that are essentially like huge documents in and of themselves.
And if you look at, you know, you go into this, you know, it's running the assignment brief.
This is like the first, just like the initial brief that we're generating.
And then it's like, okay, now go back and do research on this.
Like, I'll develop an outline that's an evolution of this assignment brief.
Okay, here's the first draft of the article.
We had the AI, even with all the artifacts that we've generated, only do the first article here.
And so it's still like, we're just starting to be more rigorous.
It's not just like, hey, GBT, write me like an article about this thing.
It's like, okay, and now like, here's a cover image, like stock cover image.
And for the research and stuff, this is all editable.
Let's go.
try.
You
And then, it's going to make me see if I can get in here.
Oops.
Oh, it's not going to let me in.
Okay.
So, like, you can inspect the underlying code, and there's also, you know, you can input manual links.
You can change the topics.
There is, in one of these workflows, it's not this one, but, like I said, you can say, like, these are competitors that you should never reference.
These are things, like, pages that you don't go to.
These are, like, CTAs that you don't use.
So, it just starts, we're starting to get much more specific because it's all built, integrated into our own platform.
So, before, we had our own code base that was, like, pointing to different parts of AI, and then we were tying it together using air ops work.

And that was like, it worked, you know, but with all of it being built on our own structure, we can start to more deeply integrate all of these pieces together and start to like develop more accurate and tonally relevant content for your teams.
Yeah, no, it's impressive.
We'll look forward to kind of getting updated, staying updated on that.
Yeah.
Yeah, because it is, there is an evolution from, you know, kind of the, yeah, it's a definite evolution, I think, from some of the other things.

Yeah, because I mean, I, I totally agree with you, like the, where things are today, and the way even like three weeks ago that we were working on generating these pieces was like, we had like a couple of static workflows, and we weren't really able to flex.
Uh, with the feedback that we were given as nimbly as we can now.
Um, and I mean, in a couple of months, I think this is gonna, we're gonna start to like, let our clients play with this.
So like, might be worth having another conversation then.
Um, but also like, given where you, like the comfort that you feel with like your in-house technical knowledge and also the like data sets that you have, like might, might not make sense.
Um, but I just wanted to kind of show you where we were going.
Um, but this was really helpful feedback.

And I think like, um, there are some things that we'll take back and think about, especially, um, in our initial calibrations and making sure that clients feel comfortable with how we're, how we're developing our technical clients.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Glad to be helpful.
it was a pleasure working with your team.
Um, you know, only.
Only good things to say.
So, yeah, keep us updated on what you all are doing.
And, yeah.
Yeah.
Thanks, Andy.
Have a good day.
You too.
Bye.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Gather feedback on GrowthX's partnership with FOSSA to improve content generation processes and understand reasons for ending the engagement.](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=1.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [FOSSA sought GrowthX's expertise to modernize and scale their content program using AI tools, aiming for faster, quality content production](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=4.0)
  - [Technical nature of FOSSA's product, need for in-house data, and non-traditional publishing process (GitHub-based) led to decision to bring content creation in-house](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=32.0)
  - [Suggestion for GrowthX: Consider offering subject matter experts for QA in specific industries to enhance content quality and client confidence](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=701.0)

## Topics

### Initial Goals and Expectations

  - [CEO connection or recommendation led to GrowthX partnership](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=95.0)
  - [Objectives:](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=82.0)
      - [Modernize and scale content program using AI tools](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=109.0)
      - [Learn from experts and jumpstart organic traffic efforts](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=127.0)
      - [No specific quantifiable metrics, but focus on repeatable, scalable AI-generated traffic assets](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=151.0)
      - [Emphasis on moving faster with quality content, both long-form editorial and PSEO tool-type assets](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=224.0)

### Challenges and Reasons for Bringing Content In-House

  - [Subject matter expertise: High bar for quality in technical product/audience](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=318.0)
  - [PSEO content quality reliant on internal data, difficult to communicate externally](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=360.0)
  - [Non-traditional content publishing process:](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=400.0)
      - [No CMS, content assets in GitHub repo](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=409.0)
      - [Manual publishing in Markdown and code](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=420.0)
  - [Inefficient to work with external agency given these factors](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=382.0)

### Current Content Generation Approach

  - [Andy using AI for \~40% of content creation](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=471.0)
  - [ChatGPT for deep research, treated as first draft](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=483.0)
  - [Not yet comfortable with 90%+ AI-generated content for longer-form editorial](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=491.0)
  - [Open to more AI involvement in PSEO content](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=525.0)

### Feedback on GrowthX Partnership

  - [Strong onboarding, despite initial competing priorities](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=258.0)
  - [Appreciated extra layer of QA for code snippets](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=661.0)
  - [Valuable learning experience, though ultimately decided in-house approach was necessary](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=284.0)

### Future Content Strategy Considerations

  - [Potential shift towards 40-50% AI involvement in content creation](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=701.0)
  - [Anticipation of increased focus on craftsmanship and quality](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=687.0)
  - [Suggestion for GrowthX: Offer true subject matter experts for technical industries](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=720.0)

### GrowthX's Evolving Platform and Workflows

  - [Transition to internal platform with custom code base](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=900.0)
  - [More specific prompting and better content control](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=1011.0)
  - [Developing company-specific artifacts (personas, writing guidelines, technical guardrails)](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=1059.0)
  - [Integration of deep research and evolving brand context](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=1145.0)
  - [Potential for more accurate and tonally relevant content generation](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=1200.0)

## Next Steps

  - [GrowthX to consider incorporating subject matter experts for QA in specific industries](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=720.0)
  - [Potential follow-up conversation in a few months to discuss GrowthX's platform evolution](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=1385.0)
  - [GrowthX to refine initial calibrations and approach for technical clients](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=1365.0)
  - [FOSSA to stay updated on GrowthX's progress and new offerings](https://fathom.video/share/x3iUhs-L3uGTJ5Cx6gyR3XqihnTqx7xz?tab=summary&timestamp=1443.0)
